# PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT
# WITHOUT PREJUDICE — FOR SETTLEMENT DISCUSSION PURPOSES ONLY (FRE 408)

## MEMORANDUM

**TO:** Sandra Koh-Wexler, Chief Executive Officer, Meridian Industrial Holdings, Inc. (cc: Rachel Fong, General Counsel)
**FROM:** David Tanaka, Kesswick, Tanaka & Moreau LLP
**DATE:** June 27, 2025
**RE:** Proposed Compromise Clause Language for the Four Contested Provisions of the Intercreditor Agreement — Meridian Industrial Holdings, Inc. — Single Integrated Package

### I. Introduction and Purpose

You asked KTM to prepare proposed compromise language for the four contested provisions of the ICA among Cartwright National Bank, N.A. (First Lien Agent), Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP (Second Lien Agent), and Meridian (Borrower), dated July 31, 2025. Negotiations commenced May 12, 2025 and have reached an impasse on: (i) Standstill Period (§3.01(b)); (ii) Second Lien Purchase Right (§5.01); (iii) Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness (§2.03(c)); and (iv) Application of Asset Disposition and Casualty Proceeds (§4.02).

For each provision this memorandum sets forth (a) full contractual drafting, (b) negotiation rationale explaining what each side gains and concedes and why the compromise falls within each side's stated flexibility, (c) citations to the KTM Market Survey of 42 1L/2L ICAs closed January 2022–December 2024, and (d) quantified economic impacts.

**The four compromises are a single, indivisible, integrated package.** Each side's willingness to move on any provision is conditioned on reciprocal movement on the others; unbundling would let either agent cherry-pick the provisions favorable to it and re-litigate the rest, collapsing the balance. Indivisibility is addressed in Section VIII.

**Urgency:** 34 days remain to the July 31, 2025 drop-dead date; the existing facility matures August 15, 2025 (15 days later), with no realistic extension. The package is designed to be closeable within this window without another round of positional bargaining.

**Privilege:** This is an attorney-client communication / work product, submitted without prejudice for settlement purposes under FRE 408. Nothing herein is an admission, concession, or binding commitment; all positions are subject to definitive documentation and to each agent's internal approvals. Flexibility signals referenced were communicated to KTM on a without-prejudice, confidential basis; this memorandum does not disclose them to the opposing side and should not be shared with either agent without first consulting KTM.

### II. Capital Structure Context and Negotiation Posture

| Metric | Value | Calculation |
|---|---|---|
| First Lien Facility (Revolver + TLA) | $475,000,000 | $125M + $350M |
| Second Lien Term Loan B | $150,000,000 | — |
| Total Secured Debt | $625,000,000 | $475M + $150M |
| Total Enterprise Value (TEV) | ~$640,000,000 | Appraisal midpoint ($620M–$660M) |
| First Lien LTV | 74.22% | $475M ÷ $640M |
| Total LTV | 97.66% | $625M ÷ $640M |
| Second Lien Equity Cushion | 25.78% ($165,000,000) | ($640M − $475M) ÷ $640M |
| First Lien Leverage | 5.31x | $475M ÷ $89.4M LTM EBITDA |
| Total Leverage | 6.99x | $625M ÷ $89.4M |
| 1L Pricing / Maturity | SOFR + 250 bps / June 15, 2030 | — |
| 2L Pricing / Maturity | SOFR + 625 bps / June 15, 2031 | — |
| SOFR (June 27, 2025) | 5.33% | For all illustrative calculations |

Two features drive the negotiation. **First**, Total LTV of 97.66% means a TEV decline of only 2.34% pushes Total LTV above 100%, at which point the Second Lien is impaired and incentives diverge. **Second**, the 25.78% cushion ($165M) is thin and is the Second Lien's primary safeguard. These pull in opposite directions on each provision, which is why a balanced package is both appropriate and achievable.

**Appraisal caveat (relevant to every provision):** the $640M TEV is a *going-concern*, debt-free/cash-free value, expressly *not* a liquidation or forced-sale value. In the distressed scenarios where the standstill, purchase right, and waterfall operate, realizable value would be materially below $640M — reinforcing both the 1L's concern about collateral erosion during a prolonged standstill *and* the 2L's concern that a prolonged standstill lets value fall below its thin cushion. This is a reason to set the standstill at the market median rather than either extreme.

**Posture:** The 1L letter (Buchanan/Aldgate, June 20) advances 1L-protective terms, grounded in the thin cushion and the seven-bank syndicate's constraints, and states Cartwright's internal standstill floor is 150 days with re-syndication required below it. The 2L letter (Oduya/Sternfeld, June 23) advances 2L-protective terms, grounded in the same cushion viewed from the junior side and in Ridgeline's fund LPA constraints (which prohibit open-ended revolving-credit commitment obligations). Each side has signaled, without prejudice, zones within which it could move; the package lands within the overlap. The Market Survey — 42 deals, 95% New York law, median 1L $475M / 2L $150M / total $625M, i.e., centered on Meridian-comparable structures — supplies the objective benchmark.

### III. The Compromise Package — Overview

| # | Section | 1L Opening | 2L Opening | **Compromise** | Market Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.01(b) | 270 days; no triggers | 90 days; 2 triggers | **180 days; 3 narrow objective triggers; cap of 2 Blockage Notices / 360 aggregate days per rolling 365-day period** | Median 180; 76% include triggers; 25 of 42 pair ≥180 days with triggers |
| 2 | 5.01 | Par + default-rate interest + fees; mandatory revolver assumption; 1L EoD only | Lesser of par (non-default) or FMV; no revolver; broader triggers | **Par for funded debt + non-default-rate interest + fees; revolver assumption optional at purchaser's election; exercisable on 1L EoD or 1L acceleration** | Par 71%; non-default rate 69%; optional revolver 62% (77% in 2024); acceleration trigger universal |
| 3 | 2.03(c) | 0% increase; maturity ≥91 days post-1L; blanket test | 20% ($30M); maturity ≥ original 2L; no test | **10% ($15M) limited to refinancing fees/costs; maturity ≥ later of (91 days post-1L) and (original 2L); narrowed test on enumerated provisions + MFN limited to financial maintenance covenants** | Median 10%; 79% limit to fees/costs; 93% use 91-day test, 81% also original-2L floor; 52% narrowed, 100% of those pair with MFN, 82% limit MFN to financial covenants |
| 4 | 4.02 | 100% hard sweep, override reinvestment; no 2L residual | Follow 1L reinvestment mechanics; residual to 2L | **Bifurcated: ordinary-course (≤$20M) follow 1L Credit Agreement §2.05(b) mechanics; large/extraordinary (>$20M) and all casualty = hard 100% sweep to 1L; anti-evasion (90-day aggregation); residual to 2L after full 1L satisfaction; quarterly + event-driven + annual reporting** | 100% sweep universal; 81% preserve reinvestment; 90% hard sweep for large; median ordinary-course $20M; 97% anti-evasion; 79% give 2L residual |

### IV. Provision 1 — Standstill Period (§3.01(b))

**A. Proposed Compromise Language**

> **Section 3.01(b) — Standstill Period.** Following the occurrence and during the continuance of a Second Lien Default, the Second Lien Agent shall not exercise any rights or remedies with respect to the Collateral for a period of one hundred eighty (180) days (the "Standstill Period") commencing on the date of actual receipt by the Second Lien Agent of a written notice (a "Blockage Notice") from the First Lien Agent specifying in reasonable detail the Second Lien Default giving rise thereto. The Standstill Period shall terminate prior to the expiration of such 180-day period upon the occurrence of any of the following early termination events, each objectively verifiable:
>
> (A) **First Lien Enforcement Inaction.** The First Lien Agent has not commenced an Enforcement Action with respect to the Collateral and has not taken any material step to enforce, collect, or realize upon the First Lien Obligations or the Collateral for a period of sixty (60) consecutive days during the Standstill Period;
>
> (B) **Disposition of Substantially All Collateral / Credit Bid.** The First Lien Agent commences a foreclosure, liquidation, or other disposition of substantially all of the Collateral, or credit bids all or substantially all of the First Lien Obligations in connection with any sale or disposition of Collateral (including under Section 363 of the Bankruptcy Code); or
>
> (C) **Impairing Plan or Sale Motion Without 2L Consent.** A plan of reorganization is filed in an Insolvency Proceeding that provides for the impairment of the Second Lien Obligations without the consent of the Second Lien Agent, or a motion is filed under Section 363 of the Bankruptcy Code seeking to sell substantially all of the assets of the Borrower and its Subsidiaries free and clear of the Liens securing the Second Lien Obligations without the consent of the Second Lien Agent.
>
> Upon any such early termination event, the Standstill Period shall immediately terminate and the Second Lien Agent may exercise any and all rights and remedies available to it under the Second Lien Loan Documents and applicable law with respect to the Collateral, subject to Section 3.03 and the priority and subordination provisions of this Agreement. There shall be no more than two (2) Blockage Notices (and no more than two (2) Standstill Periods) in effect during any rolling 365-day period, and the aggregate number of days during which a Standstill Period is in effect shall not exceed 360 days in any rolling 365-day period. If the Second Lien Default specified in a Blockage Notice is cured or waived, the Standstill Period shall immediately terminate, and any subsequent exercise of remedies shall require a new Blockage Notice and a new Standstill Period, subject to the foregoing caps.

**B. Negotiation Rationale.** *1L gains:* 180 days is above Cartwright's disclosed 150-day floor, at the market median, and within the 150–180-day range the syndicate can accept; the 60-day inaction measuring period gives the 1L a full quarter to commence enforcement before trigger A fires; the Blockage Notice cap permits a second 180-day period (up to 360 aggregate days/year) if a default cures and recurs. *1L concedes:* reduction from 270 to 180 days, plus three triggers and a cap (its opening was an absolute standstill). Supportable because 180 is above the floor and at the median, and the triggers fire only where a standstill serves no legitimate 1L purpose — when the 1L has ceased enforcement (A), is already liquidating the whole estate (B), or a plan/sale impairs the 2L without consent (C); the cap prevents converting lien subordination into indefinite payment subordination. *2L gains:* the triggers (its firm condition for any standstill over 90 days) and the cap. *2L concedes:* doubling from 90 to 180 days. Supportable because 180 is the median and is the duration the 2L has indicated it can accept *if* paired with meaningful triggers. *Within flexibility:* the 1L has signaled 150–180 days is acceptable and that a longer standstill paired with "narrow and objectively verifiable" triggers is something its committee would consider; the 2L has signaled up to 180 days is acceptable only with meaningful triggers. The package delivers exactly that.

**C. Market Data.** Median 180 days; mean 172.9; 25th pct 150; 75th pct 202.5; range 90–270. Triggers in 32 of 42 (76%); 25 of 42 pair ≥180 days with triggers ("market standard pairing"). Most common triggers: liquidation commenced (29), credit bid by 1L (22), involuntary petition (13), DIP approved over 2L objection (13), sale motion without 2L consent (9). The compromise triggers map onto this set. Deal-038 ($470M 1L/$155M 2L/$625M total, sponsor industrials — Meridian's structure) provides 240 days with a comprehensive trigger set; the compromise is more conservative. KTM's own standard-form ICA uses 180 days.

**D. Economic Quantification.** 2L time-value compensation during standstill: floating-rate 2L accrues ~$8.69M interest over 180 days ($150M × 11.58% × 180/360), substantially addressing the time-value cost. Collateral-erosion exposure: a 25.78% TEV decline zeroes 2L recovery; a 15% decline (to ~$544M) cuts 2L recovery to ~$69M (46¢ on the dollar); 180 vs. 270 days gives the 2L 90 fewer days of erosion exposure. 1L workout window: 180 days = six months, the low end of Cartwright's cited 6–9-month window, supplemented by the cap.

### V. Provision 2 — Second Lien Purchase Right (§5.01)

**A. Proposed Compromise Language**

> **Section 5.01 — Second Lien Purchase Right.** At any time following the occurrence and during the continuance of an Event of Default under the First Lien Credit Agreement, or upon the delivery by the First Lien Agent of a notice of acceleration of the First Lien Obligations, the Second Lien Agent (on behalf of the Second Lien Lenders, or any designee) shall have the right, but not the obligation (the "Second Lien Purchase Right"), to purchase all (but not less than all) of the First Lien Obligations by paying the Purchase Price equal to the sum of: (i) **Outstanding Principal** — 100% of the aggregate outstanding principal of all funded First Lien Obligations (Term Loan A + drawn Revolver); (ii) **Accrued Interest at the Non-Default Contract Rate** — all accrued and unpaid interest at the non-default contract rate (SOFR + 250 bps; 7.83% at SOFR 5.33%), through the purchase date, on a 360-day year for actual days (excluding any default-rate increment, late charges, or punitive amounts); (iii) **Fees, Costs, Expenses, and Other Amounts** — all fees, costs, expenses, indemnities then due or asserted, breakage costs, yield protection amounts, and all other amounts then due under the First Lien Loan Documents; plus (iv) **Letters of Credit** — outstanding LCs to the extent not drawn/cash-collateralized, plus cash collateralization at 103% of undrawn face.
>
> **Assumption of Unfunded Revolver Commitments — Optional.** As a condition to the purchase, the purchasing Second Lien Secured Parties may, at their sole option, assume all unfunded commitments under the First Lien Revolver ($125,000,000 aggregate, less drawn amounts) by executing an assumption agreement reasonably satisfactory to the First Lien Agent. If the purchaser elects not to assume, the unfunded Revolver commitments shall be permanently terminated (or reduced to the drawn amount) upon consummation, and the First Lien Agent and remaining Revolver lenders shall cooperate to effect such termination without impairing the Borrower's rights under then-drawn Revolver loans. The purchase shall be on a par, non-recourse, as-is, where-is basis, without representation or warranty (other than customary authority/enforceability representations).

**B. Negotiation Rationale.** *1L gains:* its non-negotiable core — par for funded debt, no FMV/dealer-poll alternative, no 2L EoD trigger (triggers limited to 1L EoD and 1L acceleration); the optional-revolver structure with mandatory-termination fallback addresses its bifurcation concern. *1L concedes:* non-default (not default) rate for accrued interest, and optional (not mandatory) revolver assumption. Supportable: non-default rate is the majority practice (69%); the 1L remains fully made whole on principal and contract-rate interest; the default-rate increment is contested on enforceability; the revolver constraint is a structural LPA limitation of Ridgeline's fund, not a posture, and the mandatory-termination fallback prevents bifurcation. *2L gains:* non-default rate (lowering price by the default increment), optional revolver (removing an LPA-prohibited $125M contingent obligation), and an acceleration trigger. *2L concedes:* par as the sole mechanic (dropping FMV), and dropping the 2L EoD trigger. Supportable: the 1L made par non-negotiable; par is the majority practice (71%); and the non-default rate + optional revolver materially lower the effective price and remove the LPA-prohibited obligation, keeping the right exercisable. *Within flexibility:* the 1L signaled par is non-negotiable but room exists on non-default rate and optional revolver; the 2L signaled it can accept par conditioned on optional revolver and is willing to discuss non-default rate. The compromise is the modal outcome for comparable structures.

**C. Market Data.** Par 71%; hybrid 19%; FMV 10%. Default-rate interest in par: only 31% (minority); 69% use non-default rate; non-default rate in 90%. Unfunded revolver: mandatory 24%, optional 62% (77% of 2024 deals), silent 14%. Acceleration trigger universal (100%); enforcement-action trigger 74%; exercise window median 30 days. Deal-007 ($475M 1L/$150M 2L/$125M Revolver, non-sponsor industrial — Meridian's exact structure): "par for funded debt at non-default rate; optional revolver; illustrative par price $484.3M" — the compromise reproduces this precisely.

**D. Economic Quantification.** Illustrative par price (funded, non-default, 90-day, full draw): $475M + $9.30M interest ($475M × 7.83% × 90/360) + ~$1.5M fees = **~$485.8M**. Value of the interest-rate concession (1L→2L): default rate is non-default + 200 bps (SOFR + 450 bps = 9.83%); the difference is **~$2.375M for 90 days** ($475M × 2.00% × 90/360) and **~$7.125M for 270 days**. Unfunded revolver: $125M; optional structure removes the contingent obligation for LPA-constrained purchasers while preserving commitment-fee economics (0.375% ≈ $469K/yr). 2L total capital at risk after exercise: ~$485.8M + $150M = **~$635.8M**, approaching the $640M TEV — the economic reality that drove the 2L's FMV ask, addressed here by lowering price via the non-default rate and removing the LPA-prohibited revolver obligation rather than discounting below par.

### VI. Provision 3 — Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness (§2.03(c))

**A. Proposed Compromise Language**

> **Section 2.03(c) — Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness** means indebtedness incurred to refinance, replace, refund, renew, or substitute all or any portion of the Second Lien Obligations, provided that: (i) **Principal Amount** — aggregate principal shall not exceed 110% of the principal being refinanced (i.e., up to $165,000,000 on the current $150,000,000, a $15,000,000 increase); provided that the portion exceeding 100% shall be limited to bona fide refinancing-related costs: (A) call premiums/make-whole/prepayment premiums, (B) arranger/underwriting/placement fees, (C) original issue discount, (D) legal/advisory/rating/filing costs, and (E) other customary transaction costs; (ii) **Maturity** — no earlier than the later of (A) 91 days after the First Lien Maturity Date (i.e., no earlier than September 14, 2030) and (B) the original Second Lien Maturity Date (i.e., no earlier than June 15, 2031); (iii) **Narrowed Restrictiveness Test** — the terms shall not be more restrictive to the Borrower, in any material respect, with respect to (A) financial maintenance covenants, (B) events of default relating to financial covenants, and (C) mandatory prepayment provisions triggered by financial metrics, than the corresponding provisions of the Second Lien Credit Agreement as in effect on the Closing Date; (iv) **Most-Favored-Nation Right** — if the refinancing indebtedness includes any financial maintenance covenant more restrictive to the Borrower than the corresponding covenant in the First Lien Credit Agreement as in effect on the Closing Date, or any financial maintenance covenant not contained in the First Lien Credit Agreement, then, at the election of the First Lien Agent (with Required Lenders consent), such covenant shall be deemed incorporated into the First Lien Credit Agreement for the benefit of the First Lien Lenders, and the Borrower shall effect such amendment without further consent (to the extent permitted); and (v) **Joinder** — holders shall join this Agreement (or a replacement on substantially similar terms) via a joinder reasonably satisfactory to both Agents.

**B. Negotiation Rationale.** *1L gains:* a restrictiveness test (which its opening demanded and the 2L's omitted), albeit narrowed; an MFN right ensuring it captures any covenant tightening in a refinancing; and its 91-day maturity test included. *1L concedes:* a 10% cap ($15M) vs. 0%, and a narrowed vs. blanket test. Supportable: 0% is the minority position (21%); median is 10%; 79% of deals with caps limit the increase to fees/costs/premiums — exactly this structure; the $15M is consumed by bona fide refinancing costs (not enterprise funding), so it does not fund operations/distributions that compress the cushion; the narrowed test targets the provisions of actual concern (financial covenants, financial-covenant EoDs, financial-metric prepayment triggers), removing the blanket test's unbounded subjective veto while the MFN preserves economic protection. *2L gains:* a 10% cap sufficient to cover customary refinancing costs (which 0% would render impracticable); a narrowed (not blanket) test preserving commercial-term flexibility; and a maturity floor anchored on the original 2L maturity. *2L concedes:* 20%→10%, and acceptance of a test + MFN (its opening was no test). Supportable: 10% is the median; the 2L signaled it can accept 10% if limited to fees/costs; the MFN is limited to financial covenants (the 82% formulation). *Within flexibility:* the 1L signaled openness to narrowed test + MFN and willingness to discuss the cap in the package; the 2L signaled 10% limited to fees/costs and openness to narrowed test + MFN. Deal-007 provides the exact formulation.

**C. Market Data.** Cap: 0% in 21%, 1–10% in 52%, 11–15% in 14%, 16–20% in 12%; median 10%, mean 8.9%; 79% of deals with caps limit to fees/costs. Maturity: 91-day test in 93%; original-2L floor also in 81%. Restrictiveness: blanket 33%, narrowed 52%, none 14%; MFN in 52%; **100% of narrowed-test deals pair with MFN**; 82% of MFNs limited to financial covenants. Narrowed+MFN grew 38% (2022) → 65% (2024); 10% cap becoming modal.

**D. Economic Quantification.** 10% cap → up to $165M; at the max, total secured debt rises $625M→$640M, Total LTV 97.66%→100.0% — the 1L's concern, which is why the increase is limited to bona fide costs. Refinancing-cost illustration (year-1 refinance): 2L soft call 2% × $150M = $3M; arranger fees 200–350 bps = $3M–$5.25M; OID 1–3% = $1.5M–$4.5M; legal/advisory ≈ $1M–$2M; total ≈ **$8.5M–$14.75M**, within the $15M cap. After year 2 (no soft call), costs drop to ~$5.5M–$11.75M. Maturity floor: no economic impact — June 15, 2031 is later than September 14, 2030, so the "later of" yields June 15, 2031, satisfying both tests; the 2L's position is *more* restrictive in practice (a clean win-win). MFN: contingent — bites only if a refinancing tightens the 2L's Total Leverage covenant (currently 6.50x) below 6.50x or adds a new financial maintenance covenant.

**E. Required Conforming Action — First Lien Credit Agreement §6.01(b).** *Critical cross-document point.* Section 6.01(b) of the First Lien Credit Agreement permits Second Lien Obligations "in an aggregate principal amount not to exceed $150,000,000 at any time outstanding." The ICA's §9.10 conflict rule governs only *as among the lien parties*; the $150M cap is a Borrower-to-1L-Lenders covenant, not an intercreditor matter, and is not overridden by the ICA as against the Borrower. **Accordingly, the 10% cap in the ICA (permitting up to $165M) will not, by itself, permit the Borrower to incur >$150M of 2L debt.** The parties must concurrently amend §6.01(b) of the First Lien Credit Agreement to permit up to $165M of Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness (or add a specific carve-out). Without this conforming amendment, the 10% cap is inert and the 2L's refinancing flexibility is no greater than under the 1L's 0% position — defeating the compromise on this provision. This amendment is a necessary component of the package.

### VII. Provision 4 — Application of Asset Disposition and Casualty Proceeds (§4.02)

**A. Proposed Compromise Language**

> **Section 4.02 — Application of Asset Disposition and Casualty Proceeds.** (a) **Bifurcated Application.** (i) **Ordinary-Course Sales (individual Net Cash Proceeds ≤ $20,000,000):** applied in accordance with the reinvestment and mandatory prepayment provisions of Section 2.05(b) of the First Lien Credit Agreement, including the $5,000,000 individual threshold, the $15,000,000 annual aggregate threshold, the 365-day reinvestment period, and the 100% sweep of non-reinvested proceeds; the Borrower retains the reinvestment rights under Section 2.05(b)(iv). (ii) **Large or Extraordinary Sales and Casualty Events (individual Net Cash Proceeds > $20,000,000; all Casualty Events):** 100% of Net Cash Proceeds applied to prepay the First Lien Obligations until the Discharge of First Lien Obligations; notwithstanding any provision of the First Lien Credit Agreement (including Section 2.05(b)(iv)), no reinvestment permitted, and no portion available for the Second Lien Obligations, the Borrower, or its equity holders until the Discharge of First Lien Obligations. (b) **Anti-Evasion.** No Asset Disposition (or series of related Asset Dispositions) shall be structured, timed, or arranged to avoid the $20,000,000 threshold; the First Lien Agent may aggregate Asset Dispositions occurring within a 90-day period if they constitute a single plan or related series, treating the aggregated proceeds as a single disposition for threshold purposes (considering the identity of the acquirer(s), the nature/location of the assets, and the timing/sequencing). (c) **Residual to Second Lien.** After application to the First Lien Obligations in accordance with (a) (including, for ordinary-course sales, after expiration of the reinvestment period and the 100% sweep of non-reinvested proceeds; and, for large/extraordinary sales and Casualty Events, after the hard 100% sweep), any residual Net Cash Proceeds shall be released to the Second Lien Agent for application to the Second Lien Obligations; provided no release prior to the Discharge of First Lien Obligations except to the extent the First Lien Agent confirms the First Lien Obligations have been paid in full or no further application is required. (d) **Reporting.** (i) quarterly certificate of compliance; (ii) prompt notice (within 5 Business Days) of any Asset Disposition or Casualty Event yielding Net Cash Proceeds above the $5,000,000 individual threshold; (iii) annual aggregate disposition report with an itemized list.

**B. Negotiation Rationale.** *1L gains:* its non-negotiable core — a hard 100% sweep of all proceeds from large/extraordinary sales (>$20M) and all Casualty Events, overriding reinvestment for those proceeds (universal market standard: 100% sweep in all 42 deals; hard sweep for large sales in 90%); plus robust anti-evasion with 90-day aggregation (97% of ordinary-course deals; mirroring the 1L Credit Agreement's own §2.05(b)(iii)). *1L concedes:* reinvestment for ordinary-course sales ≤$20M, and residual to 2L after full 1L satisfaction. Supportable: the 1L signaled reinvestment for smaller ordinary-course sales "may be acceptable" and that deferring to the 1L Credit Agreement's mechanics below a threshold is "workable"; $20M is the Survey median and the informally discussed threshold; residual-to-2L is "less of a concern" if the hard sweep is preserved and is economically limited. *2L gains:* reinvestment for ordinary-course sales (respecting the 1L Credit Agreement's negotiated mechanics, which management states are essential to maintaining the industrial asset base); some access to residual (its stated minimum, "symbolically important even if economically limited"); and anti-evasion (which it requested). *2L concedes:* a hard sweep (no reinvestment) for large/extraordinary sales and all Casualty Events, and a $20M threshold. Supportable: the 2L acknowledged hard sweeps on large/extraordinary sales "may be necessary"; $20M is the median and ≈22.4% of LTM EBITDA. *Within flexibility:* the 1L signaled the bifurcated approach is "workable" and residual is acceptable if the hard sweep is preserved; the 2L signaled it can live with the 1L mechanics for ordinary-course sales if residual flows to 2L and anti-evasion is included. Deal-007 provides the exact formulation.

**C. Market Data.** 100% sweep universal (42/42); reinvestment preserved in 81% (365-day period in 91% of those); hard sweep for large/extraordinary in 90%; ordinary-course excluded in 81%, median threshold $20M (range $10M–$30M); anti-evasion in 97% of ordinary-course deals, typically at the same threshold; 2L residual after 1L paydown in 79%; reporting — quarterly certificate 90%, prompt notice above trigger 79%, annual report 38%. The 2024 trend pairs ordinary-course reinvestment carve-out + hard sweep + anti-evasion at $20M. Deal-007 provides the exact formulation.

**D. Economic Quantification.** $20M threshold ≈ 22.4% of LTM EBITDA ($89.4M) — meaningful but bounded operational flexibility. Hard sweep and cushion: with a $165M cushion, a single large sale (e.g., $50M) swept to 1L directly reduces 1L exposure and preserves the cushion; if reinvested or flowed to 2L, the cushion is directly eroded. Residual to 2L: arises only after the $475M 1L is paid in full (requires cumulative proceeds + other paydowns > $475M against a $640M TEV) — consistent with, and not impairing, 1L priority; it merely ensures value above 1L debt flows to the next-priority secured creditor rather than being trapped at the Borrower level. Reporting: the 1L Credit Agreement already requires a Prepayment Event Notice above a $3M reporting threshold (§2.05(b)(v)); the compromise's $5M prompt-notice trigger is consistent (slightly higher), avoiding duplicative burden.

**E. Cross-Document Note — First Lien Credit Agreement §2.05(b).** The 1L Credit Agreement's §2.05(b) already contains the $5M/$15M triggers, 365-day reinvestment, 100% sweep of non-reinvested proceeds, *and* anti-evasion with 90-day aggregation (§2.05(b)(iii)). The compromise §4.02(a)(i) expressly *defers to* §2.05(b) for ordinary-course sales rather than restating the mechanics — avoiding inconsistency, avoiding the "back-door amendment" the 2L objected to, and ensuring any future §2.05(b) amendment automatically governs the ordinary-course waterfall. The ICA's own operative provisions (hard sweep for >$20M and Casualty Events, anti-evasion at $20M, residual-to-2L, reporting) are ICA-specific and control over inconsistent 1L Credit Agreement provisions as among the lien parties under §9.10.

### VIII. Package Indivisibility and Cross-Provision Trade-Offs

The four compromises are indivisible because the concessions are mutually offsetting:

1. **Standstill duration (1L-favorable) ↔ triggers + Blockage Notice cap (2L-favorable).** The 1L's acceptance of 180 days (above its floor, at the median) is the consideration for the 2L's acceptance of three triggers and a cap; the 2L's acceptance of 180 days (double its opening) is conditioned on the triggers and cap. Neither stands alone.

2. **Par purchase price (1L-favorable) ↔ non-default rate + optional revolver (2L-favorable).** The 2L's par concession (dropping FMV) is the consideration for the 1L's interest-rate and revolver concessions. The optional-revolver structure is, in addition, a structural necessity for Ridgeline given its LPA constraints — not a concession the 1L can re-trade elsewhere without collapsing the purchase right's viability for the very party it is designed to benefit.

3. **10% cap + narrowed test (1L-favorable on the test; 2L-favorable on the cap) ↔ MFN (1L-favorable) and "later of" maturity (win-win).** The 1L's acceptance of a 10% upsize and a narrowed test is the consideration for the 2L's acceptance of a test and an MFN. The maturity "later of" formulation costs neither side anything. The 10% cap is, in turn, dependent on the conforming amendment to 1L Credit Agreement §6.01(b) (Section VI.E) — without it, the cap is inert and the balance collapses.

4. **Hard sweep for large/extraordinary sales and Casualty Events (1L-favorable) ↔ ordinary-course reinvestment + residual to 2L (2L-favorable).** The 1L's non-negotiable hard sweep is preserved; the 2L's minimum (reinvestment + residual) is preserved; anti-evasion and reporting protect both.

**Why indivisibility matters:** if unbundled, each agent would accept the provisions favorable to it (1L: 180-day standstill, par, restrictiveness test, hard sweep; 2L: triggers, non-default rate, optional revolver, 10% cap, ordinary-course reinvestment, residual) while re-litigating the rest — returning the parties to the impasse and consuming the remaining 34 days. Presenting the package as indivisible, with Meridian endorsing it as a coherent whole to both agents, is the mechanism most likely to produce closure. Meridian is uniquely positioned to make this credible: both agents recognize the Borrower needs both lender groups committed and closing on time, and neither side can walk away.

### IX. Cross-Cutting Observations and Required Conforming Actions

1. **Conforming amendment to 1L Credit Agreement §6.01(b) (required).** As detailed in Section VI.E, the 10% cap is inert unless §6.01(b) is concurrently amended to permit up to $165M of Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness. This is the single most important cross-document action item and a necessary component of the package.

2. **Cross-reference to 1L Credit Agreement §2.05(b) (recommended).** §4.02(a)(i) defers to §2.05(b) for ordinary-course sales, avoiding inconsistency and back-door amendment; preserve this in definitive drafting.

3. **Maturity floor is a non-issue (win-win).** The "later of" formulation resolves the dispute at no economic cost; the 2L's position is more restrictive in practice. Present as already resolved in principle.

4. **Financial-covenant headroom (relevant to MFN and restrictiveness test).** 1L covenant: First Lien Net Leverage ≤ 5.50x; Closing Date 1L leverage ≈ 5.31x (only ~0.19x headroom). 2L covenant: Total Leverage ≤ 6.50x; Closing Date Total Leverage ≈ 6.99x (above the cap, though not tested until the first full quarter). Headroom is thin at both levels; any tightening of the 2L's Total Leverage covenant in a refinancing (the MFN scenario) could push the Borrower into default. The compromise's covenant provisions are calibrated to this reality.

5. **Appraisal going-concern caveat (relevant to standstill and waterfall).** The $640M TEV is going-concern, not liquidation value; in distress, realizable value is materially lower — supporting a median (180-day) standstill and a hard sweep for large sales while permitting ordinary-course reinvestment.

6. **Consistency with KTM's standard-form ICA.** KTM's house form provides a 180-day standstill (§3.1), a par purchase price (§5.2), and a proceeds waterfall deferring to the 1L Credit Agreement's mandatory prepayment provisions with residual to the 2L (§4.2). The package is consistent with the house form, giving both agents comfort that it reflects a workable, market-standard structure.

7. **Governing law.** All documents are New York law; the compromise is drafted consistently, including enforcement of subordination under §510(a) of the Bankruptcy Code and application of NY GOL §§5-1401/5-1402.

### X. Recommendations and Next Steps

1. **Meridian endorsement.** Signal Meridian's support for the package as a single, indivisible whole to both Cartwright (via Buchanan/Aldgate) and Ridgeline (via Oduya/Sternfeld), promptly, noting the package lands at the market median on each point and within each side's stated flexibility. Endorse as a coherent package, not provision-by-provision.

2. **Conforming amendment to 1L Credit Agreement §6.01(b).** Direct KTM to prepare, for circulation with the package, a conforming amendment permitting up to $165M of Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness, presented to the 1L Agent as a necessary component of the package.

3. **Joint drafting session.** Propose a joint session (in person or video) with both agents' counsel within 5 Business Days to walk through the package provision-by-provision and produce a single agreed mark-up. Because each provision is calibrated to the median and to each side's flexibility, the session should focus on confirming drafting rather than re-litigating positions.

4. **Internal approvals.** Ask both agents to commence credit-committee (Cartwright/syndicate) and fund-governance (Ridgeline) approvals in parallel with the drafting session, on the basis of the package as presented. Alignment with the market median should facilitate approvals.

5. **Reservation of rights.** Until the ICA and the conforming amendment are executed, all positions remain without prejudice and subject to definitive documentation; Meridian should take no action in reliance on the package prior to execution.
